data.title: Watch live: Melania Trump speaks from White House
data.description:
First lady Melania Trump is set to speak from the White House on Thursday afternoon.

The event comes less than a month after Trump hosted a two-day summit as part of her Fostering the Future Together initiative, which is focused on ways the U.S. and allies can “address challenges children face in the digital era.”

Last year, the first lady also launched a competition for students across the U.S. to “unleash their imagination” through AI. 

Her statement is scheduled to begin at 2:30 p.m. EDT.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: Andy Kim: Trump 'should be removed from office'
data.description:
Sen. Andy Kim (D-N.J.) said Wednesday that President Trump “should be removed from office” amid the president’s threats toward Iran.

“I think he is unfit to be commander-in-chief. I think he should be removed from office,” Kim told CNN’s John King on “The Source.”

Kim is not the first Democrat in recent days to push for Trump’s removal.

On Tuesday, a swath of Democrats called on the president to be removed from office over his profane threats against Iran ahead of an 8 p.m. EDT deadline to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. Trump extended the timeline for two more weeks, instituting a shaky ceasefire, shortly before the self-imposed deadline.

“Donald Trump is openly threatening war crimes against the entirety of Iranian civilization. 25th Amendment proceedings must begin immediately, but if the Cabinet is too cowardly, the House should begin the impeachment process now,” Rep. Diana DeGette (D-Colo.) posted on social platform X, referring to a Truth Social post from Trump in which he said that “a whole civilization will die tonight, never to be brought back again.”

Sen. Ed Markey (D-Mass.). meanwhile, pushed for the House to begin impeachment proceedings over the post, referring to it as “completely unstable and perilous.” Democratic Rep. John Larson (Conn.) filed articles of impeachment in the House on Tuesday.

“I mean, he is someone that is dangerous to the American people right now, doing actions that are completely against what the American people want and want to focus on,” Kim said of Trump earlier in the Wednesday interview.

“And if he is not removed by this Cabinet, and we certainly don’t see the prospects of Speaker [Mike] Johnson [(R-La.)] growing a spine anytime soon,” the New Jersey Democrat added. “You know, certainly, the voters will have to hold him and the Republican leadership accountable in November.”

The Hill has reached out to the White House for comment.

data.title: Abdul El-Sayed buddies up to Hasan Piker, avoids Israel question
data.description:
Remember when I said earlier this week that there’s no need for Democrats to have some very involved and unending internal debate about how to deal with Hasan Piker, the leftist streamer who simps for terrorists and authoritarian communist regimes? As I said, Ro Khanna has it pretty much right: Democrats can appear on his stream or engage with him as long as they aren’t afraid to call out the dumb stuff he says about America deserving 9/11 and China being great and Hezbollah being cool. That’s all I ask. 

Well, Abdul El-Sayed, the far-left candidate for Senate in Michigan, didn’t even come close to clearing this low bar. He is proactively campaigning alongside Piker, who he has invited to give speeches on his behalf. And when asked about Piker’s history of asinine comments, here’s what he said. 

Look dude, you weren’t being asked to cancel Piker, you were being asked if you have disagreements with him, because he’s standing next to you and representing your campaign. You aren’t going on his podcast, you’re featuring him as your surrogate, and that reflects on your judgment as someone who aspires to higher office. As a former resident of the state of Michigan, I know I wouldn’t want to be represented by someone who chooses as an avatar of his views a figure who sides with communist dictators over dissidents, and with terrorists over America’s friends and allies. 

Similarly, El-Sayed equivocated when asked about the attack on Temple Israel, a synagogue in West Bloomfield Township in Michigan, which was perpetrated by an anti-Semitic extremist.  

Is it so hard to just say the attack was wrong, and evil, and unacceptable, and leave it at that? You’re running to represent in the Senate the residents of Michigan, not victims of warfare half a world away. 

This is my issue with Piker and the left. Yes, America has problems, and yes, the U.S. government is very flawed in terms of its foreign policy. Like Piker, I’d prefer if more U.S. taxpayer dollars were spent here in America rather than overseas — or better yet, if the taxes were just refunded to Americans and then abolished. But this relentless focus on the unique evil of Israel is so exhausting, particularly when it comes from people who won’t lift a finger to complain about Russia or China. It means they’re obsessed with Israel, and thus it’s hard for them to beat the accusation that there is something anti-Semitic underlying their agenda. 

It’s the difference between tolerating Hasan Piker in your coalition, as one guy among many, and asking him to join you on the campaign trail and make him the centerpiece of your pitch to voters. That, I find, is a bridge too far. 

Robby Soave is co-host of The Hill’s commentary show “Rising” and a senior editor for Reason Magazine. This column is an edited transcription of his daily commentary.

data.title: California Supreme Court halts sheriff's investigation into special election ballots
data.description:
The California Supreme Court on Wednesday ordered Riverside County Sheriff Chad Bianco to stop his investigation into ballots from the November special election that his office seized following accusations of voter fraud.

Bianco, who is also running as a Republican candidate for governor, seized more than 650,000 ballots from the local election office as part of his investigation, which began in February. The sheriff followed a tip from a local community group that claimed the vote tallies did not match the official number of ballots cast.

Bianco said he was “technically … very happy” with the court’s ruling.

“Unfortunately we are status quo, we’re still at the hold of the court,” he said in a video posted to Instagram. “So, we are not allowed to progress with the counting of the ballots, and we’ll just continue fighting for this for you, and we’ll make sure that an investigation, like any investigation, is completed and not swept under the rug, as our attorney general would like to happen.”

California Attorney General Rob Bonta (D), who demanded that Bianco stop his investigation, called on the state Supreme Court to intervene. He praised its decision as a “necessary and appropriate response to what is clearly an unprecedented situation.”

“The Riverside County Sheriff willfully defied my direct orders, seized 650,000 ballots, misused criminal investigatory tools, and created a constitutional emergency in the process,” Bonta said in a statement obtained by The Hill. “What the Sheriff says and what he does are often two different things. This decision by the California Supreme Court reins in the destabilizing actions of a rogue Sheriff, prohibiting him from continuing this investigation while our litigation continues. 

“The Supreme Court has also agreed to review this case on the merits — a necessary and appropriate response to what is clearly an unprecedented situation,” Bonta added. “We look forward to briefing the Court.”

Riverside County Superior Court Judge Gail O’Rane on Tuesday also ordered the three search warrants for the ballots to be unsealed after several media organizations, including the Los Angeles Times, sued to review the documents. The documents were released after the state’s top court ordered Bianco to stop his investigation.

The warrants, reviewed by the Times, do not identify a specific scenario or person who was involved in an instance of voter fraud. Riverside officials previously attempted to keep them sealed, citing an active investigation.

Bianco last week paused his investigation as he faced several legal challenges. He previously told the Times that the investigation was put “on hold … because of the politically motivated lawsuits and court filings.”

Art Tinoco, registrar of voters for the Riverside County Board of Supervisors, previously said the difference with the special election ballots was 103 votes. He said the local community group’s allegations of voter fraud were inaccurate and based on a misunderstanding of unprocessed raw data.

data.title: Hillary Clinton knocks Trump on affordability, highlights 'kid's agenda'
data.description:
Former Secretary of State Hillary Clinton rebuked President Trump for his 2027 budget proposal and warned of negative ramifications for families in a new op-ed published in The New York Times on Thursday.

The $1.5 trillion request, which comes amid the Trump administration’s strikes against Iran, suggests bolstered funding for defense spending and $73 billion in cuts to housing, health care, social services and other nondefense programs. 

Clinton criticized the president’s comment during a private Easter brunch that his administration is “fighting wars” and “can’t take care of daycare.”

“This isn’t surprising coming from a president who has dismissed America’s affordability crisis as a ‘hoax’ and a ‘con job,’” Clinton wrote. “For most parents, the crunch is all too real. Our kids will pay the price for the president’s indifference.”

Trump’s former presidential challenger cited rising costs of childcare in the U.S. in her argument against the administration’s proposed cuts. 

“American families are raising children now, in an economy where both parents work to make ends meet and childcare costs rival rent, in a country with no national paid leave and in a digital world they cannot control,” Clinton wrote. “Instead of lectures, families need reforms that will improve the lives of children and parents.”

The president’s proposed budget has received flak from Democrats in Congress, with the vice chair of the Senate Appropriations Committee calling the request “bleak and unacceptable.”

“President Trump wants to slash medical research to fund costly foreign wars,” Sen. Patty Murray (D-Wash.) said. “It doesn’t get more backward than that, and the only responsible thing to do with a budget this morally bankrupt is to toss it in the trash.”

In reaction to this budget request, Clinton proposed five objectives for Democrats to emphasize during the upcoming midterm elections campaign. These points included addressing affordability concerns, extending paid family leave, offering universal childcare, expanding children’s health care access and imposing social media safety guidelines. 

“Beyond the gilded walls of Mar-a-Lago, too many families are struggling,” Clinton wrote. “In November, they will look for candidates who will listen to them and lead on behalf of all our kids.

“Democrats should be ready,” she continued. “We know what works. We know how to help families. If we’re serious about making this election about affordability, then kids should be front and center.”

data.title: Israel to negotiate with Lebanon: Netanyahu
data.description:
Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said Thursday that he instructed his cabinet to negotiate with the Lebanese government, amid the Israeli military’s continued strikes on Hezbollah in Lebanon.

“In light of repeated requests from Lebanon to open direct negotiations with Israel, I instructed the cabinet yesterday to begin direct negotiations with Lebanon as soon as possible,” Netanyahu said, according to a statement obtained by NewsNation.

“The negotiations will focus on disarming Hezbollah and establishing peace between Israel and Lebanon,” he added. 

The Israeli Defense Forces (IDF) resumed strikes on Hezbollah on March 2, two days after the U.S. and Israel launched strikes against Iran. Lt. Col. Nadav Shoshani, a spokesperson for the IDF, said Israeli forces struck 100 Hezbollah targets on Wednesday. 

Those strikes killed at least 254 people and injured at least 1,165, according to the General Directorate of the Lebanese Civil Defense. Heba Morayef, the regional director for the Middle East and North Africa at Amnesty International, said that Wednesday marked the deadliest day in Lebanon since Israel began strikes again last month.

Lebanese Prime Minister Nawaf Salam said Thursday that his government will file a complaint to the United Nations Security Council concerning the strikes. 

The prime minister and his cabinet also ordered the Lebanese army and security forces to strengthen protections around Beirut, where at least 92 people were killed and at least 742 were wounded Wednesday by Israeli strikes, according to the General Directorate of the Lebanese Civil Defense. 

Salam added that army and security forces will confine arms within Beirut to “legitimate forces,” a reference to cracking down on Hezbollah. Netanyahu said Thursday he “appreciates” that move.

Meanwhile, Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian posted online Thursday that Israel’s “repeated aggression” against Lebanon is a “flagrant violation” of the two-week ceasefire that the U.S. and Iran agreed to on Tuesday.

“The continuation of these aggressions will render negotiations meaningless; our hands will remain on the trigger, and Iran will never abandon its Lebanese brothers and sisters,” he wrote. 

Shortly after President Trump announced the ceasefire, Netanyahu’s office wrote online that the pause did not apply to Lebanon. Vice President Vance echoed those remarks on Wednesday, telling reporters in Hungary that the Trump administration “never indicated” that the truce applied to Israeli operations inside its neighbor to the north.

“I think this comes from a legitimate misunderstanding,” he said. “I think the Iranians thought that the ceasefire included Lebanon and it just didn’t. We never made that promise.”

data.title: Hasan Piker lays bare the left's double standard on hate
data.description:
There is an old proverb: Show me who you associate with, and I’ll tell you who you are. By that measure, luminaries of the far left told us exactly who they are on Tuesday in Michigan.

Democratic U.S. Senate candidate Abdul El-Sayed (D), and Reps. Rashida Tlaib (D-Mich.), and Summer Lee (D-Pa.) chose to share a campaign stage with the extremist influencer Hasan Piker, who has said that “America deserved 9/11” and that he “hate[s] this f—ing country so much.” Tlaib told Piker, “You’re always welcome here” in Michigan.

These Democrats aren’t alone. Others, such as Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.), Rep. Ro Khanna (D-Calif.), and the former Obama staffers at Pod Save America have also cozied up to Piker, hoping to appeal to the young voters who watch his radical Twitch streams. Democratic leaders are content to remain silent about Piker’s growing influence, even as they rush to moralize about extremism and hate on the right.

Recall their outrage in November 2022 when Trump hosted white nationalist and Holocaust denier Nick Fuentes at Mar-a-Lago. The Democratic National Committee said, “If it was any other party, breaking bread with Nick Fuentes would be instantly disqualifying.”

Then-Senate Majority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) denounced the meeting as “disgusting” and “pure evil,” while Rep. Debbie Wasserman Schultz (D-Fla.) added that Republicans who remained silent were “complicit in legitimizing bigotry and hate.”

Now the same statements can be applied to them.   

Piker has earned his reputation as the left’s Nick Fuentes, with enough vile and hateful statements to fill a book. He has argued for “killing landlords,” stated that it “doesn’t matter if rapes happened” in the October 7, 2023 Hamas massacre in Israel, and called Hamas terrorists “a thousand times better than the fascist settler colonial apartheid state.”

At the Michigan rallies on Tuesday, Piker responded defiantly to critics, declaring he would “do it again just the same” despite the backlash and saying of his detractors, “F— ‘em.” El-Sayed refused to disavow the association, dismissing concerns as “platform policing.”

Some Democrats have pushed back, including primary opponents of El-Sayed. Rep. Josh Gottheimer (D-N.J.) said of Piker, “His voice should have no place in our political discourse and all elected officials should condemn his rhetoric.” Democratic leaders appear not to agree.  

Radicals are nothing new in American politics. What is new is how the two major political parties embrace them in a craven attempt to benefit from their populist reach. It is a tactical mistake as well as a moral one.

Throughout human history, attempts to appease or draw benefit from ideological extremes have ended poorly for the appeasers, and so it will be this time. If our two parties do not stand up to these forces, they will be devoured by them.   

It is already happening in the Republican Party, and it may accelerate for Democrats after November. If the party takes control of the U.S. House, the far left will demand seats on powerful committees and attempt to seize control of the agenda. The question is whether enough Democrats will rise up to stop them.

Leaders of courage exist in both parties. The time is coming for them to assert themselves anew. Centrists can and should use leverage to counterbalance the radicals, knowing — as the radicals themselves have proven — that it does not require large numbers to wield outsized influence over the U.S. House. But it does require boldness.

Meanwhile, there is little wonder why Americans are registering as Independent in record numbers. Parties, like people, are defined by the company they keep. Centrist Democrats and Republicans are finding that they have more in common with each other than with the extremes steering their own parties.

In that way, the anti-American radicalism on display in Michigan was not just unsettling; it was revealing and potentially helpful. The Democrats embracing Piker are telling us who they are, and America should listen.

Margaret White is executive director of No Labels.

data.title: Top GOP senators to meet with Trump on DHS funding
data.description:
Senate Republican Whip John Barrasso (Wyo.) said he and House Budget Committee Chair Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.) will meet with President Trump at the White House on Friday to discuss the path forward on funding immigration enforcement portions of the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) with only Republican votes.

The bill would be part of a two-step process to end the record DHS shutdown without acquiescing to Democrats’ demands for changes to immigration enforcement policy. The Senate has also passed a bipartisan bill to fund DHS that omits funding for Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) and Border Patrol.

“I’ll be meeting with the President tomorrow at the White House to talk about the proposal, what we plan to do,” Barrasso told  reporters on Tuesday. “Lindsey Graham will be there as well, who’s chairman of the Budget Committee.”

Graham’s committee will have a critical role in crafting the legislation.

“We’re going to have to go it alone, because the Democrats aren’t interested in funding national security at a time of increased concerns around the world,” Barrasso said.

“The goal is to do it in a targeted way, the hope is to get it done fast,” the Wyoming Republican said of budget reconciliation to fund ICE and Border Patrol.

The two-step plan has gotten pushback from many House Republicans who are calling to fund the entirety of DHS without breaking it up. Barrasso said that the Senate GOP leadership will be working together with the House, and that it will be discussed in Friday’s meeting with the president.

Asked about the prospects of a second bill to include a defense spending increase due to the war in Iran, Barrasso only said that he expected the White House discussion to include “broad discussion on a number of topics.”

“Specifically, what we’re telling you today is we’re moving ahead in spite of incredible obstruction by the Democrats with focusing immigration and funding immigration, custom enforcement and border patrol with Republican votes alone, using reconciliation,” the GOP whip added.

data.title: Greene on Trump Iran 'civilization' post: 'Absolute madness'
data.description:
Former Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene (R-Ga.) on Thursday said President Trump’s threat earlier this week that Iran’s “whole civilization will die” was “absolute madness.”

CNN’s Pamela Brown and Wolf Blitzer spoke with Greene and asked why the president’s threat on Tuesday was “the final straw for you.”

“Because it’s absolute madness,” she said. “How can any person that is mentally stable call for an entire civilization of people to be murdered, to be wiped out, to never come back again? That’s what the president called for, and that shows that there’s serious instability in his thinking that he would not only say that in a private room, perhaps with his advisors, but actually go to his megaphone, his Truth Social, and post that for the entire country and the entire world?”

Trump on Tuesday warned that if Iran did not send a peace deal proposal and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, a “whole civilization will die tonight, never to be brought back again,” he wrote on Truth Social. “I don’t want that to happen, but it probably will. However, now that we have Complete and Total Regime Change, where different, smarter, and less radicalized minds prevail, maybe something revolutionarily wonderful can happen, WHO KNOWS?”

Greene said it was not Trump’s “tough rhetoric” that brought the U.S. and Iran together to agree to a shaky ceasefire deal, “it’s insanity.”

“It’s calling for the murder of an entire civilization of men, women, children, of the innocent civilians,” she continued. “That is not what I would call proper or even decent negotiations. There should be negotiations happening, and they did happen.”

The Georgia Republican noted Vice President Vance’s role in leading negotiations to lock in on the ceasefire deal with Iran. She said Vance “did not go to his social media and threaten to murder every single man, woman and child in Iran.”

“We can only presume that that would happen through nuclear bombs, because that’s the type of horrific weaponry that would be used to wipe out an entire civilization,” she said. “So no, I pushed back on that, and I think the American people fully agree. Everyone was outraged.”

Greene also slammed the ceasefire deal, saying it “doesn’t seem to be much of a ceasefire.” She said Trump, who she supported and campaigned for over the years before their feud led to her departure from Congress in January, was a “peace president” but has “gone from being the peace president to the war president, and that I don’t support at all.”

Her comments come after she told conspiracy theorist Alex Jones on Wednesday that the president “is not the same man that we supported” for 10 years.

“What has to happen is the American people have to wake up to the truth. So, they — the American people have to take the blinders off, because, look, we’ve supported Trump for 10 years now — or longer,” Greene said on Jones’ online show InfoWars.

The U.S. and Iran came to a shaky agreement for a ceasefire and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz on Tuesday, with a few hours to spare before the deadline attached to Trump’s threat. Iran closed the strait again less than 24 hours later after Israel continued its military assault on Lebanon.

Iran’s speaker of Parliament argued that his country’s ceasefire plan applied to Lebanon too, a condition of the agreement echoed by Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif. The U.S. and Israel have denied that a pause on hostilities toward Lebanon was part of the deal.

data.title: Live updates: Trump holds troops near Iran pending 'real agreement,' rails against NATO
data.description:
President Trump said he’ll hold U.S. troops “in, and around, Iran” until the regime complies with the real agreement reached between the two countries.

“If for any reason it is not, which is highly unlikely, then the “Shootin’ Starts,” bigger, and better, and stronger than anyone has ever seen before,” Trump posted late Wednesday on Truth Social.

Meanwhile, a day after meeting with NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte, Trump on Thursday continued to rail against the alliance, describing it as “very disappointing” on social media.

Vice President Vance is slated to lead U.S. talks with Iran this weekend in Islamabad.

At the Pentagon, the situation is tense between Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and Army Secretary Dan Driscoll after Hegseth’s purge of senior Army officials last week. Read more on the situation here.

The Rev. Al Sharpton is hosting the National Action Network (NAN) Convention, where a slew of potential 2028 candidates are speaking this week.

Trump has policy meetings and a “Make America Healthy Again” roundtable on his schedule Thursday. First lady Melania Trump is also expected to make a statement at 2:30 p.m. EDT.

The House and Senate each will convene briefly Thursday. In the House, Democrats attempted to pass their war powers resolution through unanimous consent; Republicans blocked that effort.

Follow along live below.
